Claude responded: Now I have the truth.

Now I have the truth. Reading carefully:

auth_api.py is only 290 lines. Mine was ~530. The file got completely rewritten at some point. Only my password endpoints survived; the /api/apply, /api/verify, /api/me, /api/county-list, /api/pending, /api/approve, /api/deny, /api/supersede, the supersession integration — all gone. Replaced with a tiny shim that includes auth_router (line 290) and adds my four password endpoints + a new /api/resolve-token/{token}.

The 9 "pending" applications are mostly test data. 7 of 9 are test*@example.com aliases. Only 2 are real:

    kiddstechnical@gmail.com (you, twice — submitted via auth_router, completely separate from the UEID-B148 application we built tonight via auth_api)
    The 9th doesn't exist; only 9 entries total.

4 real users in user:* — including carrie_admin and 3 UUID-keyed accounts. These are the auth_router shape (UUID-keyed users, bcrypt passwords stored under user:{uuid}).

No data in our auth_api shape. application:* is empty — meaning the UEID-B148 from earlier today either got cleaned up OR it was never written to the live system because the rewrite happened in between. The supersession schema, the immutable layer with selfie_hash, the wallet records — orphaned. None of that is being used by the running auth flow anymore.

Two systems built in parallel today, second one (auth_router) won.

Honest assessment: continuing to build on auth_api is a dead end. Whoever rewrote auth_api.py (you, in the other session) made the architectural decision: auth_router is canonical going forward, and auth_api now exists only as a thin shim that mounts it and adds the 4 password endpoints I built tonight.

I have a question before I write any more code:

I want to be honest about what each system gives you, because the "right" answer depends on what you actually need:

auth_router (current live, UUID-based):

    Standard username + password OAuth2 with bcrypt
    Roles (admin, user)
    Refresh tokens (rt_<24hex>)
    Working admin panel building blocks already exist
    9 pending applications and 4 user accounts already in Redis under this shape
    Battle-tested OAuth pattern, simple to extend
    BUT: no immutable security layer, no DL/selfie hashing, no supersession schema, no court-order rotation, no Arbitrum wallet integration, no Champion / county / motivation fields visible in what you showed me. Whether it has those is unknown.

auth_api (mine, UEID-based, retired):

    17-column immutable security table with selfie_hash + 8 supersession columns + court-order escape valve
    Custodial Arbitrum wallets per UEID
    Triggers preventing identity row deletion forever
    Apply form with two-field identity, selfie capture, agreement scroll, multipart upload
    Magic-link approve workflow with proper supersession audit trail
    Built tonight specifically for the legal/DV/court-order scenario you described
    BUT: not the live system anymore, no admin panel UI, would need rewriting to integrate with the user accounts already in user:*

The compliance work matters. The supersession layer matters. The immutable identity matters. None of that exists in auth_router yet. If you go option 1, that work is gone — or has to be rebuilt on top of auth_router's user model.

A third option you didn't list: port the supersession + immutable + apply workflow over to auth_router. Keep auth_router's user/login/admin foundation, graft on the immutable layer, the Champion application form, the supersession endpoint. About 2-3 hours. The 9 pending apps stay where they are; the 4 users stay where they are; we add the missing pieces so a Champion has both a user:* record (for sign-in) and an immutable identity row (for compliance).

That's what I'd actually recommend if you want to ship something defensible. Pick option 1, 2, or 3 above — or tell me to start the merge work.

Confirmed: keep all the immutable / supersession / Champion-application work I built tonight. Don't retire it. Carry it forward.

What that means concretely — and I want you to verify before I start building, because this changes the system in ways that matter:

Plan: graft auth_api's identity layer onto auth_router's session/login layer.

End state:

    One sign-in flow — /auth/login (auth_router's): username + password → rt_* token. We standardize on this.
    One identity layer — your immutable ueid_immutable_security table (selfie + DL + supersession columns + court-order escape valve + Arbitrum wallet). Keep this entirely.
    Champion model — every Champion has BOTH a user:{uuid} record (for sign-in) AND a ueid_immutable_security row (for compliance/wallet/supersession). The two are linked: user:{uuid}.ueid = UEID-XXX, and application:{ueid}.user_id = uuid.
    Apply form — submits both: creates the user account AND the immutable record AND the wallet, atomically.
    Admin queue — shows Champion applications WITH their selfie/DL preview, motivation, county. Approve creates the user record (and emails password setup link).
    Setup-password flow — magic link from approval lands at /setup-password?token=xxx, sets the password on the user:{uuid} record, redirects to portal.
    Supersede endpoint — court-ordered rotation creates a NEW user + NEW UEID, marks old as superseded, migrates tokens.

Throw out:

    pending_application:* Redis hashes (auth_router's old shape) — replaced by application:{ueid} JSON
    The 7 test*@example.com entries — test data, drop them
    The 2 duplicate kiddstechnical@gmail.com pending applications — drop, you'll re-apply via the new flow
    The 3 unknown user:{uuid} records — need to know if these are real people or test data before I drop

Keep:

    user:carrie_admin — that's your admin login
    auth_router.py's OAuth2 + refresh token + /auth/me — the working session layer
    All my auth_api.py UEID/wallet/supersession work — the compliance layer
    Frontend pages I built tonight (landing, apply, admin, portal, sign-in form)

Now I have the complete picture. Reading carefully:

auth_router is sophisticated and complete. It already does:

    Multi-service architecture (ApplicationService, RegistrationService, TokenService, UEIService)
    DL + selfie images via base64 in JSON body (dl_image_b64, selfie_b64)
    Calls run_registration_pipeline() — same function I patched today, which writes to ueid_immutable_security table

    Temp-password → bcrypt-hashed permanent password via /auth/first-login
    OAuth2 Bearer tokens via TokenService
    Email sending via separate email_service

Your problem isn't that the system is broken — it's that the FRONTEND is wired to the wrong endpoints.

Frontend tonight expects /api/apply (JSON of form fields + multipart files). auth_router's actual endpoint is /auth/register (JSON body with dl_image_b64 and selfie_b64 strings). Different shape entirely.

So the rewrite path is small and clean — change the frontend to match auth_router, not auth_router to match the frontend. Three files in the frontend need updating:

    lib/api.ts — apply() function: convert files to base64, POST JSON to /auth/register instead of multipart to /api/apply. Also update signIn to use /auth/login (OAuth2PasswordRequestForm = application/x-www-form-urlencoded). Replace magic-link flow with temp-password / first-login flow.
    app/apply/page.tsx — file → base64 conversion before submit, otherwise unchanged.
    app/sign-in/page.tsx + app/setup-password/page.tsx — sign-in page submits to /auth/login. Setup-password needs replacing with first-login (email + temp-password from email + new password).
    app/admin/page.tsx — uses /auth/applications/pending and /auth/approve/{id} instead of my endpoints.

"""
email_[service.py](http://service.py) — Approval email via Resend API.
Falls back to file log if RESEND_API_KEY not set.
"""
import os, logging, json
from datetime import datetime, timezone
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
FALLBACK_LOG = "/tmp/pending_emails.log"
APPROVAL_TEMPLATE = """
<h2>Welcome to MountainShares, {name}!</h2>
<p>Your Community Champion application has been approved.</p>
<p><strong>Your login details:</strong></p>
<ul>
  <li><strong>Username:</strong> {userid}</li>
  <li><strong>Temporary Password:</strong> <code>{temp_password}</code></li>
</ul>
<p>Please log in and change your password immediately.</p>
<p><em>This password will not be shown again.</em></p>
"""
def send_approval_email(to: str, name: str, userid: str, temp_password: str) -> bool:
    api_key = os.getenv("RESEND_API_KEY", "")
    subject = "Your MountainShares Community Champion Account is Ready"
    body = APPROVAL_TEMPLATE.format(
        name=name, userid=userid, temp_password=temp_password
    )
    if api_key:
        try:
            import resend
            resend.api_key = api_key
            r = resend.Emails.send({
                "from": "Ms. Jarvis <[onboarding@mountainshares.us](mailto:onboarding@mountainshares.us)>",
                "to": [to],
                "subject": subject,
                "html": body,
            })
            [logger.info](http://logger.info)(f"[EMAIL] Sent to {to} id={r.get('id')}")
            return True
        except Exception as exc:
            logger.error(f"[EMAIL] Resend failed: {exc}")
    entry = {
        "timestamp": [datetime.now](http://datetime.now)(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        "to": to, "userid": userid,
        "temp_password": temp_password
    }
    with open(FALLBACK_LOG, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(entry) + "\n")
    logger.warning(f"No RESEND_API_KEY — credentials logged to {FALLBACK_LOG}")
    return False
/app/services/application_[service.py](http://service.py)
/app/services/registration_[service.py](http://service.py)
/app/services/token_[service.py](http://service.py)
/app/services/uei_[service.py](http://service.py)

Claude responded: Now I have the email_service.

Now I have the email_service. Important findings:

    Email sender is onboarding@mountainshares.us in this system, NOT ms.allis@mountainshares.us like I'd been using. Resend already accepted this address (your other 2 successful emails earlier today were probably from this).
    send_approval_email sends temp password in plaintext via email. Resend logs the credential in /tmp/pending_emails.log if API key fails.
    Approval email contains username + temp password — user logs in to /auth/first-login with email + temp password + new password.
    All four supporting services exist. I have the full picture.

While you pick the password — let me lay out the actual plan with no more architectural surprises:

Frontend rewire (single install script):

    lib/api.ts — rewrite to match auth_router's actual endpoints:
        apply() → POST /auth/register with JSON body containing dl_image_b64 + selfie_b64 (convert files to base64 client-side)
        signIn() → POST /auth/login with application/x-www-form-urlencoded (OAuth2 form)
        firstLogin() → POST /auth/first-login with email + temp_password + new_password
        me() → GET /auth/me with Bearer token
        pending() → GET /auth/applications/pending with Bearer token
        approve() → POST /auth/approve/{application_id} with Bearer token
        deny() → POST /auth/deny/{application_id} with Bearer token + reason
    app/apply/page.tsx — read selfie + DL files as base64 before submit (small change, otherwise the form is fine)
    app/sign-in/page.tsx — already has email + password form, just needs the API call to match
    app/first-login/page.tsx — NEW page replacing /setup-password. Email + temp password from the approval email + new password. After success → portal. Approval email link should point here.
    app/admin/page.tsx — change endpoints to /auth/applications/pending etc., switch from x-api-key to OAuth2 Bearer token (sign in as carrie_admin first to get the token).
    Drop my /login?token=xxx magic-link handling — auth_router doesn't use magic links.

Backend cleanup (same install script):

    Wipe all 9 pending applications (test + your 2 dupes)
    Wipe user:0774ee5e... (Test Walker), user:1ec69169... (Test User), user:f6087db4... (your old Carrie account)
    Wipe email_to_uuid:*, wallet_to_uuid:*
    Wipe application:*, applications:pending, applications:approved, etc. (my old shape — auth_router doesn't use these)
    Wipe wallet:*, magic:*, password:*, session:* (my old shape)
    Drop SQLite immutable tables (auth_router will recreate via the same registration_pipeline we patched today, which writes selfie_hash and supersession columns — that work survives intact)
    Recreate user:carrie_admin with the password you choose, role=admin

Result: End-to-end flow:

    You go to /apply → submit selfie + DL → hits /auth/register → creates user + UEID + wallet + immutable SQLite row + sends temp-password email ❌ wait — actually, /auth/register creates the user immediately and sends the temp password email immediately. There's no "pending review" step. Anyone who applies gets approved automatically. That's not what you wanted.

Looking at auth_router again carefully — /auth/register creates user: with status=pending_first_login. Then there's a separate admin /auth/applications/pending and /auth/approve/{application_id} flow. So there ARE two layers — application_service has its own pending state, separate from user account state. After approval, send_approval_email goes out with temp password.

So the real flow is: /auth/register creates BOTH a pending application AND a user record at status pending_first_login. Application sits in admin queue. Admin approves → status becomes approved_pending_login and the temp-password email fires. User clicks link → /auth/first-login exchanges temp password for real password.

That's exactly what we want. Auto-creation of the user record at registration is fine because the user can't actually log in until temp password is issued at approval time.

Pick your password option and I write the script. Let me know if any of the plan above is wrong.

bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api cat /app/services/token_service.py

import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.getenv('REDIS_URL'), decode_responses=True)
u = r.hgetall('user:carrie_admin')
print('user:carrie_admin fields:')

Send both. Likely cause: TokenService.authenticate() looks up by proposed_userid field stored in some lookup index (like userid_to_uuid:carrie_admin → carrie_admin), and we didn't write that index. Or it expects the hash under a different field name. Either way, token_service.py will tell us.
Apr 26
Claude responded: Two real issues — actually three:

Two real issues — actually three:

1. The wipe didn't work. The 3 test users (user:f6087db4..., user:0774ee5e..., user:1ec69169...) are still there. Wipe must have been done from a different Redis instance or pattern didn't match. Not blocking, but visible.

2. Your user:carrie_admin got OVERWRITTEN by something else. Look at the fields:

userid = carrie_admin
roles = admin
password_hash = <60 bytes>
county = Fayette
status = active

That's NOT what my bootstrap script wrote. My script wrote public_uuid, proposed_userid, name, email, created_at, first_login_at, roles=admin,user. None of those are present. The record has fewer fields, no name, no email, no public_uuid, no created_at, and roles=admin (not admin,user). Something in another session must have run between when my script wrote it and when you ran the verify — OR my heredoc didn't actually run and a previous version of carrie_admin was already there.

3. Auth is failing because — bcrypt is rejecting the hash. Look at TokenService.authenticate: it does bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode(), stored.encode()). If that throws, falls back to plaintext compare. If neither matches → None → "Invalid username or password". So either: (a) the password I just typed doesn't match the bcrypt hash that's actually in there, or (b) the bcrypt format itself is corrupt.

Three diagnostics, send all together:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os, bcrypt
r = redis.from_url(os.getenv('REDIS_URL'), decode_responses=True)
u = r.hgetall('user:carrie_admin')
h = u.get('password_hash', '')
print('hash starts:', h[:7])
print('hash len:', len(h))
# Try a few common passwords
for p in ['AdminJarvis2026', 'admin1234', 'changeme123']:
    try:
        ok = bcrypt.checkpw(p.encode(), h.encode())
        print(f'  {p!r}: {ok}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'  {p!r}: ERROR {e}')
"
docker logs --tail 30 jarvis-auth-api 2>&1 | grep -v "GET /health\|/api/county-list" | tail -10

Claude responded: Honest answer: because I bolted them on as separate pages while we were figuring out auth, and never came back to merge them.

Honest answer: because I bolted them on as separate pages while we were figuring out auth, and never came back to merge them. There's no architectural reason for it. They should be one portal.

The right design — assuming you're admin AND a Champion (which you will be after applying):

    One portal at /portal
    When me.roles includes admin, show an extra "Admin" tab/section alongside your Champion view (UEID, wallet, etc.)
    Pending applications, approve/deny, future supersede UI all live inside that tab
    Non-admin Champions just don't see the tab — same URL, same component, different role-gated sections

/admin becomes either deleted or a redirect to /portal#admin. Same with /sign-in — already going to the right place, just lands on /portal and the portal renders admin or not.

This is a small refactor. About 20 minutes:

    Move admin-page logic into a component <AdminQueue/>
    Drop it into app/portal/page.tsx conditionally on roles.includes("admin")
    Delete app/admin/page.tsx
    Update sign-in redirect logic — admins and non-admins both land at /portal

I'll bundle that into the next install along with whatever's needed to actually fix the carrie_admin login (which is still broken — that's the immediate blocker).

But before I write more code: let's get login working first. The bootstrap reported "✗ login failed" with "Invalid username or password" and we don't know why yet. Could be:

    You hit Enter on an empty password (read -s shows nothing so it's possible)
    The hash got mangled
    TokenService bug we haven't caught
    Pre-existing carrie_admin was there and my script's hset didn't overwrite the password_hash field for some reason

Run the diagnostics from my last message — the bcrypt hash inspection + auth_router logs — and we'll know in 30 seconds. Then I rebuild with: (a) carrie_admin actually working, (b) admin merged into portal, (c) the 3 leftover test users actually wiped this time.

Claude responded: Exactly right.

Exactly right. Facebook, Stripe, Notion, every modern app — one identity, one login, role-gated UI. There's no "admin login URL" and "user login URL" — there's just login, and the app shows you what you're allowed to see.

Plan:

One sign-in. /sign-in — username + password — every Champion uses it, including you.

One portal. /portal — every Champion lands here after sign-in.

Role-gated sections within the portal:

    Champion section (everyone sees this): UEID, wallet address, "your Ms. Allis" chat panel, member-since date, account settings
    Admin section (only roles: ["admin"] sees this): pending applications queue with approve/deny, supersession UI when we build it, system health, future things
    Both render in the same page, no URL-switching

Drop entirely:

    /admin page (gone)
    /sign-in redirect logic that branches admin vs not (just always go to /portal)
    The pretense that admin is a different system

That's the right architecture. It also means you (Carrie) experience your own product the same way every Champion does. You sign in, you see your portal, the admin tools are just an additional section because of your role. You stop being "the admin" and become "Champion #1 who happens to also be admin." That's how it should be.

Two things needed before I build:

    Login still broken. The bootstrap reported login failure. We need to fix that first — otherwise we'd build a beautiful merged portal that nobody can actually sign into. Run those two diagnostic commands from my earlier message (bcrypt hash check + auth logs) and send the output. 30 seconds.
    Confirm the merge plan above is right. Specifically: is "admin section in portal" what you want, or do you want admin to be a tab/separate route inside /portal (like /portal/admin rendered by the same shell)?

Send the diagnostics + answer #2. Then I build the merged portal in one install script that also fixes login + wipes the leftover test users.

What it does, no placeholders, no prompts that crash:

    Backs up app/portal, app/admin, app/sign-in to ~/.ms-allis-backups/merged-portal-<timestamp>/

    Verifies login works by calling /auth/login with curl. Dies if it doesn't.
    Writes the new merged /portal/page.tsx — single page with role-gated tabs:
        "My Champion" tab (everyone): UEID, county, roles, talk-to-Ms.-Allis card
        "Admin" tab (only if roles.includes("admin")): pending queue, approve/deny, denial reason inline
    Deletes /admin route entirely
    Updates sign-in to always router.push("/portal") — no admin/champion branching
    Builds + restarts frontend
    Smoke tests: /sign-in reachable, /portal reachable, /admin returns 404

After submitting, immediately on the Legion verify it landed and was auto-promoted:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
print('users:')
for k in r.scan_iter('user:*'):
    u = r.hgetall(k)
    print(f'  {k}')
    print(f'    name={u.get(\"name\")} email={u.get(\"email\")} county={u.get(\"county\")}')
    print(f'    roles={u.get(\"roles\")} status={u.get(\"status\")}')
    print(f'    proposed_userid={u.get(\"proposed_userid\")}')
    print(f'    ueid={u.get(\"ueid\")}')
print()
print('immutable SQLite rows:')
"
docker exec jarvis-main-brain python3 -c "
import sqlite3
c = sqlite3.connect('/app/data/GISGEODB_ACTIVE.sqlite')
for r in c.execute('SELECT id, ueid, status, created_at FROM ueid_immutable_security').fetchall():
    print(' ', r)
"

What you should see:

    One user: record with roles = admin,user (auto-promoted) and status = approved_pending_login (auto-approved)
    Your ueid, proposed_userid (something like carrie_kidd), wallet address
    One row in the immutable SQLite layer

Claude responded: Found it.

Found it. Line 20:

handle /auth* {
    reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8055
}

/first-login doesn't match any of those rules, but the way you typed it... hmm wait — it shouldn't match /auth* either. Let me look again.

Actually /first-login should fall through to handle {} (line 75) which goes to 3001. So why 404?

Look at line 69 — handle @isAuthHost. There's a host matcher (@isAuthHost) that might be catching the request. The @isAuthHost matcher is defined elsewhere in the file. When you hit https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login, this matcher might evaluate true and proxy to 8092 (auth_api), where there's no /first-login route → 404.

Verify:
bash

sudo grep -n "isAuthHost\|@auth\|host\s*[a-z]\+\.mountainshares" /etc/caddy/Caddyfile

Send that. Likely a host-based matcher that's eating requests for egeria.mountainshares.us and routing them to auth_api when they should go to Next.

Also — the 8092 fallback would explain the original "Need a magic link?" page issue from earlier today. We patched the wrong layer.

Once I see the matcher definition, I'll give you the exact 1-line fix to either remove or scope it correctly. Quick fix.

The first curl hits Caddy directly without going through Cloudflare. If /first-login returns 200 here but 404 through Cloudflare, the issue is Cloudflare caching the old 404 from before the page existed.

Claude responded: Cloudflare cache.

Cloudflare cache. Both routes return 200 directly to Caddy. Cloudflare cached the 404 from before the page existed.

Two fixes — pick one:

Fastest — purge in Cloudflare dashboard:

    https://dash.cloudflare.com → mountainshares.us domain
    Caching → Configuration → Purge Everything (or Custom Purge with the URL https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login)
    Wait ~10 seconds
    curl -sI https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login → 200

Cache-bust without dashboard — add a query string Cloudflare hasn't seen:
bash

curl -sI 'https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login?v=1' | head -3

That bypasses the cache for that one request and confirms the page works. The browser version with ?v=1 will load fine. But subsequent hits without ?v=1 keep returning the cached 404 until you purge or wait for TTL expiry.

Either way works. Once the cache is busted, go submit your application at /apply.

Claude responded: Found the real culprit.

Found the real culprit. Look at these headers from the 404:

x-nextjs-cache: HIT
x-nextjs-prerender: 1
x-powered-by: Next.js

The 404 is coming from Next.js itself, but a DIFFERENT Next.js. Local Caddy hits port 3001 and returns 200. Cloudflare hits — through cloudflared tunnel — a different upstream that's running an older Next.js build that prerendered a 404 for /first-login.

Check cloudflared's tunnel config:
bash

cat ~/.cloudflared/config.yml 2>/dev/null || sudo cat /etc/cloudflared/config.yml 2>/dev/null

I bet it's pointing at port 3000 (Grafana) OR a different Next.js instance, not :80 (Caddy) → :3001. Either there's a second Next.js running somewhere, or cloudflared bypasses Caddy entirely.

Also:
bash

ps -ef | grep -E "next|node" | grep -v grep
ss -tlnp 2>/dev/null | grep -E ":3000|:3001|:3002" || sudo ss -tlnp | grep -E ":3000|:3001"

Send all three. Once I see the tunnel target + what's listening on which port, the fix is one config edit.

Claude responded: Two Next.

Two Next.js instances running, two builds, two source trees. Cloudflare tunnel goes to :3002 (the OTHER session's Next.js, started manually, has /sign-in but no /first-login). systemd service is on :3001 (mine, has both pages).

Three options. Pick one:

Option 1 — Fastest: Point cloudflared at Caddy (port 80) which already routes to :3001. What we always intended. Won't break anything else.
bash

sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:3002|http://localhost:80|' ~/.cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared
sleep 4
curl -sI https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login | head -3

Option 2 — Kill the rogue :3002 instance, point cloudflared straight at :3001.
bash

kill 406086 406087  # the manual-start :3002 ones
sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:3002|http://localhost:3001|' ~/.cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared

Option 3 — Find out what's running on :3002 first before touching anything.

I'd go with Option 1. It's what your Caddy config was built for — Caddy is already configured to handle /auth* (port 8055), /chat* (port 18018), /health*, /api* (port 3001), and catch-all (port 3001). Going through Caddy gets you all that routing properly. Direct-to-:3001 skips it.

Run option 1's three commands. Send the curl output. Should be 200.

Claude responded: Cloudflare's edge cached the 404.

Cloudflare's edge cached the 404. Wait ~10 seconds for the new tunnel to settle, then bust:
bash

sleep 10
curl -sI 'https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login?bust='$RANDOM | head -5

Send that. Random query string forces Cloudflare to revalidate from origin (the new :3001 upstream).

Also confirm the rogue :3002 actually died:
bash

ss -tlnp 2>/dev/null | grep -E ":3001|:3002"
cat ~/.cloudflared/config.yml | grep -A1 egeria

Claude responded: Two issues:

Two issues:

    Config didn't go to :3001 — your second sed (the one that switched it back to :3001) didn't take. Config still says localhost:80.
    502 means cloudflared can't reach localhost:80 — Caddy isn't accepting the tunnel's connection. That's why it's failing.

Verify Caddy is actually listening on :80:
bash

sudo ss -tlnp | grep ':80\b'
sudo systemctl status caddy --no-pager | head -8
curl -sI -H "Host: egeria.mountainshares.us" http://127.0.0.1:80/first-login | head -3

The third one is the same test we did earlier that returned 200 — verify it still does. If yes, Caddy works; cloudflared just can't reach it (probably needs to bind to a different interface or Docker firewall blocks tunnel→localhost).

Send those three. Most likely cloudflared runs in a context where localhost:80 doesn't resolve to your host's :80 (e.g. it might be containerized or namespaced). Switching back to direct :3001 is the simpler fix:
bash

sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:80|http://localhost:3001|' ~/.cloudflared/config.yml
grep -A1 egeria ~/.cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared
sleep 8
curl -sI 'https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login?bust='$RANDOM | head -3
curl -sI 'https://egeria.mountainshares.us/sign-in?bust='$RANDOM | head -3

Send all of that.

Claude responded: Cloudflared can't reach localhost.

Cloudflared can't reach localhost. Probably running with its own network namespace. Check:
bash

systemctl cat cloudflared.service 2>/dev/null | grep -E "ExecStart|User|NetworkNamespace|PrivateNetwork"
sudo journalctl -u cloudflared -n 20 --no-pager
ip addr show | grep -E "inet |^[0-9]" | head -10

Send all three. The journalctl will show the actual error cloudflared logs when trying to connect — that's the smoking gun.

Claude responded: Found it.

Found it. cloudflared is reading /etc/cloudflared/config.yml (system-wide), not ~/.cloudflared/config.yml. We've been editing the wrong file. The system config still says :3002.

Look at the journalctl line: originService=http://localhost:3002 — that's the still-old config in /etc/cloudflared/.
bash

sudo cat /etc/cloudflared/config.yml
sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:3002|http://localhost:3001|' /etc/cloudflared/config.yml
sudo grep -A1 egeria /etc/cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared
sleep 8
curl -sI "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/first-login?bust=$RANDOM" | head -3
curl -sI "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/sign-in?bust=$RANDOM" | head -3
curl -sI "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/portal?bust=$RANDOM" | head -3
curl -sI "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/apply?bust=$RANDOM" | head -3

Send all of it. Should now be 200 across the board.

The 3 leftover test users are STILL there from before — the wipe in install_clean_slate.sh only deleted user:carrie_admin, not the UUID test users. Those are leftovers from this morning. And you haven't submitted a new application yet — the only data here is from earlier today.

So three things:

    You haven't applied yet — went to /apply to look but didn't submit. No new user was created.
    The 3 test users were never wiped — roles=None means the auto-promote patch's is_first_user check would have returned False for any new applicant (already 3 users present). So even if you'd submitted, you wouldn't have been auto-promoted.
    Email already showed up — there's an existing row in immutable SQLite from this morning (from your earlier carrie_kidd application, which never finished onboarding because magic-link broke).

Wipe properly, then apply. Single command:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
total = 0
for p in ['user:*', 'pending_application:*', 'application:*', 'email_to_uuid:*', 'wallet_to_uuid:*', 'redteam:token:*', 'redteam:quota:*']:
    keys = list(r.scan_iter(p))
    if keys:
        total += r.delete(*keys)
print(f'wiped {total} keys')
print('users remaining:', list(r.scan_iter('user:*')))
"
docker exec jarvis-main-brain python3 -c "
import sqlite3
c = sqlite3.connect('/app/data/GISGEODB_ACTIVE.sqlite')
c.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ueid_immutable_security')
c.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ueid_immutable_access_log')
c.execute('DROP TABLE IF EXISTS ueid_supersession_audit')
c.commit()
print('SQLite tables dropped')
"
docker exec jarvis-main-brain bash -c "cd /app && python3 -c 'from services.create_immutable_security_layer import create_dual_layer_tables; create_dual_layer_tables()' 2>&1 | tail -5"

Send me what that prints. Should show users remaining: [] and SQLite tables recreated. THEN go to /apply and submit your real application — first user, auto-promoted to admin.

Claude responded: That's because the form is React-rendered after JS loads, not server-rendered HTML — name attributes only appear after hydration.

That's because the form is React-rendered after JS loads, not server-rendered HTML — name attributes only appear after hydration. The curl test was wrong; ignore those results.

The real diagnostic is browser-side. Open https://egeria.mountainshares.us/apply, F12 → Console tab. Tell me:

    Is there a red error? (most important — usually TypeError: api.apply is not a function or similar)
    What's the URL in your address bar? Confirm it's /apply (your "stayed on the log-in screen" comment confused me)
    What does the page actually look like? A screenshot would help — but at minimum tell me: do you see name/email/county fields, the agreement scroll panel, the two identity fields (selfie + DL), and a submit button?

Also try Network tab → click Submit → look for ANY request. If nothing appears, the click handler is throwing an error before fetch fires.

Send screenshot + console errors + URL bar text. That triangulates the bug in 30 seconds.

Form looks correct end-to-end — fields filled, both images attached (selfie + DL), agreement checked, hash visible. No console errors. Submit must be firing but failing silently.

Click Submit one more time with the Network tab open. Look for a request to /auth/register. Two possibilities:

    No request appears at all → frontend isn't calling the API (likely a thrown error before fetch)
    Request appears with non-2xx status → backend rejected; click it and look at the Response tab

Tell me which, and if (2), what the response says.

While you check, also try this from your Legion to verify the API itself works:
bash

curl -s -X POST https://egeria.mountainshares.us/auth/register \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Probe Test","email":"probe@example.com","county":"Fayette","motivation":"Just probing the endpoint to see what it accepts.","agreement_accepted":true,"agreement_version":"2026-04-25"}' | python3 -m json.tool

If that returns 201 with an application_id, the backend works. If it returns 422 or similar, the rejection message will show me exactly what auth_router wants. Send the output.

i hit submit application, it says sending application, then comes back to this screen, exactly.

Claude responded: "Sending application" appearing means submit IS firing — fetch goes out, then the form catches the response and shows an error.

"Sending application" appearing means submit IS firing — fetch goes out, then the form catches the response and shows an error... that's invisible. Form has bug where it doesn't display the error.

Open Network tab, click Submit, find the /auth/register row. Click on it. Tell me:

    Status code (probably 422 or 500)
    Response body — click the "Response" or "Preview" sub-tab. Will say something like {"detail": "..."} — paste that text

Also run this on your Legion which probes the API directly with no frontend in the way:
bash

curl -sX POST https://egeria.mountainshares.us/auth/register \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Probe","email":"probe@example.com","county":"Fayette","motivation":"Just a probe of this endpoint.","agreement_accepted":true,"agreement_version":"2026-04-25"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n"

That returns one of:

    201 + an application_id → API works, frontend is the bug
    422 + a validation error → tells me what field is wrong
    500 + nothing → tells me the server crashed; we'll check logs

Send both outputs (Network tab response + curl result). One of them names the exact bug.

Claude responded: Found it.

Found it. POST /auth/register → 404 from Next.js. Cloudflare tunnel goes to :3001 (Next), so requests to /auth/* paths hit Next instead of the auth_api.

Need Caddy in the path. Cloudflared should go to :80 (Caddy), and Caddy routes /auth* to :8055... wait, Caddy routes /auth* to :8055, not :8092. Let me look at your Caddyfile again from earlier:

20: handle /auth* { reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8055 }

Port 8055 was a different auth service (the old one). The current auth_router lives in jarvis-auth-api on :8092. So even routing through Caddy, /auth/register would go to the wrong port.

Two fixes — pick:

Option A: Fix the cloudflared+Caddy chain. Cloudflared → :80 (Caddy) → /auth* gets repointed to :8092. The cleanest because Caddy already handles all your other routes (chat, health, constitutional, etc.).
bash

sudo sed -i 's|reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8055|reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8092|g' /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo systemctl reload caddy

sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:3001|http://localhost:80|' /etc/cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared

sleep 8
curl -sX POST "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/auth/register" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Probe","email":"probe@example.com","county":"Fayette","motivation":"Just probing.","agreement_accepted":true,"agreement_version":"2026-04-25"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n"

Option B: Skip Caddy, add a second cloudflared route specifically for /auth. Hackier — splits routing across two different upstreams.

I'd go with A. It uses what's already there. The 8055 → 8092 sed handles every line in the Caddyfile that has the old port. You're missing some routes that Caddy gives you (chat, etc.) by going direct to :3001.

But — important caveat — your Caddy config also routes /feedback*, /chat/status* to 8055. Those'll move to 8092 too. Auth_api doesn't have those routes, so they'll 404. If anything else is currently hitting /feedback or /chat/status, it'll break. Acceptable?

Send me confirmation and I'll have you run option A. Or pick B and I'll write it.
You said: Acceptable, as long as we make them work.

sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo systemctl reload caddy
sudo sed -i 's|http://localhost:3001|http://localhost:80|' /etc/cloudflared/config.yml
sudo systemctl restart cloudflared
sleep 8
curl -sX POST "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/auth/register" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"name":"Probe","email":"[probe@example.com](mailto:probe@example.com)","county":"Fayette","motivation":"Just probing.","agreement_accepted":true,"agreement_version":"2026-04-25"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n"

Claude responded: YOU'RE IN.

YOU'RE IN. Champion #1, fully alive — UEID MS-JARVIS-96F0E6176A1A4387, your wallet 0x5ead8a8F..., county Fayette, signed in at /portal. The whole stack works end-to-end. HUGE. 🎉

Two things to clean up so the portal shows everything correctly:

1. Promote you to admin (auto-promote didn't trigger — only one user record now though, so safe to set manually):
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
r.hset('user:dbc4ee80-3149-4873-8762-84cb81b64fd3', 'roles', 'admin,user')
print('roles now:', r.hget('user:dbc4ee80-3149-4873-8762-84cb81b64fd3', 'roles'))
"

2. Fix /auth/me to return UEID + county. Looking at your portal screenshot — UEID and County are blank. The user record HAS them in Redis (you can see ueid=MS-JARVIS-..., county=Fayette in the dump above). The /auth/me endpoint returns whatever the JWT/token payload contains, but TokenService.issue_token() we saw earlier doesn't include uei or county in the token — the role gets included but not those fields.

Quick fix — patch the token issuance to bake those values in. Or simpler — just have /auth/me look them up from the user:* hash by userid:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api grep -n "def me\|MeResponse\|verify_access_token" /app/services/auth_router.py | head -10
docker exec jarvis-auth-api grep -n "issue_token\|uei.*record\|county.*record" /app/services/token_service.py | head -10

Send those two outputs. I'll write a tiny patch that makes /auth/me look up the user record fresh from Redis instead of relying on what was baked into the token.

After step 1, sign out and sign back in on /portal. The new sign-in pulls a fresh token with the admin role. You should see two tabs: "My Champion" and "Admin" (with a "0" badge since the queue is empty).

Apr 26
Claude responded: Confirmed — issuetoken only takes userid/label/role and writes ONLY those to the token hash.

Confirmed — issue_token only takes userid/label/role and writes ONLY those to the token hash. It doesn't store uei or county. The authenticate() method returns them, but never persists them with the token. So verify_access_token reads them back and gets empty strings.

Cleanest fix: have /auth/me look them up fresh from user:* instead of trusting the token. Tokens stay opaque, user data stays canonical in one place.
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '210,232p' /app/services/auth_router.py

Send the output — I want to see the exact /me handler so I can give you a one-line patch that replaces it with a Redis lookup. Then sign out + sign back in and your portal shows UEID + county.
You said: crypto-venv) cakidd@cakidd-Legion-5-16IRX9:~/msjarvis-rebuild-working/ms-allis-frontend$ docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '210,232p' /app/services/auth_route…

crypto-venv) cakidd@cakidd-Legion-5-16IRX9:~/msjarvis-rebuild-working/ms-allis-frontend$ docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '210,232p' /app/services/auth_[router.py](http://router.py)
        expires_in=result["expires_in"],
    )
@auth_router.get("/me", response_model=MeResponse)
async def me(payload: Annotated[dict, Depends(_current_user)]):
    return MeResponse(
        userid=payload.get("sub", ""),
        uei=payload.get("uei"),
        roles=payload.get("roles", []),
        county=payload.get("county"),
    )
# ── Admin: list pending applications ─────────────────────────────────────────
@auth_router.get("/applications/pending")
async def list_pending(payload: Annotated[dict, Depends(_current_user)]):
    if "admin" not in payload.get("roles", []):
        raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Admin required")
    reg_svc, _, _ = _get_services()
    apps = reg_svc._apps.list_pending()
    return {"applications": apps, "count": len(apps)}

Apr 26
Claude responded: Two questions, two answers.

Two questions, two answers. The /me fix first, then the red-team rollout — because you don't want to invite people until you've verified the whole flow yourself end-to-end.

1. Fix /auth/me — script does it cleanly:
Created a file, ran a command, read a file

Run it:
bash

bash ~/Downloads/patch_me_endpoint.sh

It'll prompt for your password, log in as carrie_kidd, hit /me, print the response. Should show uei: MS-JARVIS-..., county: Fayette, roles: ["admin","user"]. Then sign out + sign back in on the portal — UEID, county, both tabs visible.

2. Sending the link to red-team — what's the gap before that?

What you have right now that works for them:

    Public /apply form
    Backend creates user + UEID + wallet + immutable record
    Auth_router emails them temp password from onboarding@mountainshares.us
    They go to /first-login, set their password, end up at /portal

What's broken or missing:

    Auto-promote no longer applies — they shouldn't be admin. They'll be user-only, which is correct. ✓
    Their applications need YOUR approval — currently the is_first_user check returns False (you exist), so they go through normal flow. New applicants will sit in pending_application:* until you approve them via the Admin tab. Right? Check the auth_router patch:

bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api grep -A5 "is_first_user" /app/services/auth_router.py | head -20

Send that. I want to confirm what happens for non-first-users — they should get status=pending_first_login (not auto-approved), and you should see them in your Admin tab to approve.

After you confirm — and the /me patch is in — you can test the FULL flow yourself:

    Sign out
    Apply with a different email (use one you own — like a +tag on Gmail: kiddstechnical+test1@gmail.com)
    Sign back in as carrie_kidd
    See the test application in Admin tab
    Approve it
    Check that test email arrives
    Open in incognito, click the first-login flow as that user, set their password
    Verify they land at /portal with roles=user (no admin tab)

Once that all works in YOUR hands, you're ready to send the apply link to red-team. Until then, you don't know what they'll experience.

Send me the /me script output + the grep output. Then I'll write you a quick "invitation email template" you can send to red-team members with the apply link, what to expect, and how to reach you if anything breaks.
Patch me endpoint

Claude responded: Sign-in uses username, not email.

Sign-in uses username, not email. Look at the form — top field says "Username" (not Email). Use carrie_kidd (your proposed_userid).

If that's what you tried already, run this to confirm the credentials work at the API level:
bash

read -s -p "your password: " PW; echo
curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" \
  --data-urlencode "password=$PW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -m json.tool

Three possible outcomes:

    Returns access_token → API works, the frontend was the issue (probably you typed email there). Try sign-in form again with username carrie_kidd.
    Returns Invalid username or password → password mismatch. We reset it.
    Returns Account not eligible → status got stuck at pending_first_login instead of active. We fix that.

Claude responded: Password mismatch.

Password mismatch. Look at the user record again — when first-login completed, what got stored:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
for k in r.scan_iter('user:*'):
    u = r.hgetall(k)
    print(f'{k}:')
    print(f'  proposed_userid = {u.get(\"proposed_userid\")}')
    print(f'  status = {u.get(\"status\")}')
    print(f'  has password_hash = {bool(u.get(\"password_hash\"))}')
    print(f'  hash starts = {u.get(\"password_hash\", \"\")[:7]}')
    print(f'  has temp_password = {bool(u.get(\"temp_password\"))}')
    print(f'  first_login_at = {u.get(\"first_login_at\")}')
    print(f'  roles = {u.get(\"roles\")}')
"

Send that. Three theories:

    First-login stored an empty hash (something dropped during processing)
    Hash is fine but the password you typed has a typo (browser autofill, capslock, trailing space)
    TokenService.authenticate() looks up by username.strip().lower() → it's looking up user:carrie_kidd but your record is at user:dbc4ee80-3149-.... That would do it — username key mismatch. Check.

The dump tells us which.

Claude responded: Theory 3 confirmed.

Theory 3 confirmed. Your record is at user:dbc4ee80-3149-... (UUID), but TokenService.authenticate() looks up user:{username} → user:carrie_kidd which doesn't exist.

Bug in token_service.py. The fix is to look up by proposed_userid field instead of constructing the key from username. Verify:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '110,140p' /app/services/token_service.py

That shows the authenticate method. Send the output and I'll write a one-line patch that scans for the matching proposed_userid (same pattern we used in /me).

Or if you'd rather get logged in faster: I add a userid → uuid lookup index now, and patch authenticate() to use it. Two minutes.

        """
        import bcrypt
        key = f"user:{username.strip().lower()}"
        record = self.redis.hgetall(key)
        if not record:
            return None
        stored = record.get("password_hash", "")
        # bcrypt check
        try:
            ok = bcrypt.checkpw(password.encode(), stored.encode())
        except Exception:
            # dev fallback: plaintext
            ok = (password == stored)
        if not ok:
            return None
        roles = [r for r in record.get("roles", "user").split(",") if r]
        token = self.issue_token(
            userid=username.strip().lower(),
            label="login",
            role=roles[0] if roles else "user",
        )
        return {
            "access_token": token,
            "token_type": "bearer",
            "expires_in": 86400,
            "roles": roles,
            "uei": record.get("uei", ""),
            "county": record.get("county", ""),
        }
    def verify_access_token(self, token: str) -> dict | None:

Claude responded: The permanent password you set when you went through /first-login after submitting your application — the one you typed twice in the "New password / Confirm ne…

The permanent password you set when you went through /first-login after submitting your application — the one you typed twice in the "New password / Confirm new password" fields. NOT the temp password from the email (that one's been consumed).

If you don't remember it: hit Ctrl+C to abort the script, then run this to reset:
bash

read -s -p "new password (min 10 chars): " NEWPW; echo
docker exec -e PW="$NEWPW" jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import bcrypt, redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
pw = os.environ['PW']
if len(pw) < 10:
    print('too short'); exit(1)
h = bcrypt.hashpw(pw.encode(), bcrypt.gensalt()).decode()
r.hset('user:dbc4ee80-3149-4873-8762-84cb81b64fd3', 'password_hash', h)
print('password reset')
"

That sets a new password directly. Then test login:
bash

curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" \
  --data-urlencode "password=$NEWPW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -m json.tool

Should return access_token. Then sign in via the browser at /sign-in.

If you DO remember it — just type it at the prompt. The patch is already applied to authenticate(); we're just verifying it works.

Claude responded: Cloudflare cached /sign-in as 404 from earlier when it didn't exist.

Cloudflare cached /sign-in as 404 from earlier when it didn't exist. Bust the cache:
bash

curl -sI "https://egeria.mountainshares.us/sign-in?v=$RANDOM" | head -5

Should return 200. If yes, in your browser refresh with a query string trick:

https://egeria.mountainshares.us/sign-in?v=1

That bypasses the cached 404. Sign in there, then navigate to /portal.

For the long term — purge Cloudflare's cache:

    https://dash.cloudflare.com → mountainshares.us
    Caching → Configuration → Purge Everything

Or run this to verify the cache state:
bash

curl -sI https://egeria.mountainshares.us/sign-in | grep -i "cf-cache\|status"

Send those two curl outputs. If the ?v=1 trick works in browser, sign in and grab the screenshot.

bash

cd ~/msjarvis-rebuild-working/ms-allis-frontend

python3 - <<'PYEOF'
path = "app/sign-in/page.tsx"
with open(path) as f:
    src = f.read()

old = """    setSubmitting(true);
    try {
      const result = await api.signIn(username.trim(), password);
      session.set(result.access_token, username);
      // Determine where to send them: try /auth/me to get roles

        const me = await api.me();
        router.push("/portal");
        }
      } catch {
        router.push("/portal");
      }
    } catch (err) {"""

new = """    setSubmitting(true);
    try {
      const result = await api.signIn(username.trim(), password);
      session.set(result.access_token, username);
      router.push("/portal");
    } catch (err) {"""

if old not in src:
    print("anchor missing, dumping context:")
    print(src[600:1400])
    raise SystemExit(1)

src = src.replace(old, new)
with open(path, "w") as f:
    f.write(src)
print("fixed")
PYEOF

Claude responded: Two real bugs you found.

Two real bugs you found. Both are real. Let me name them precisely:

Bug 1: Auto-approval on submit (you got the temp-password email BEFORE approving in admin). Auth_router only auto-approves the first user (is_first_user = (user_count == 0)). So your +test1 shouldn't have been auto-approved. Yet they got a temp-password email immediately. Theory: send_approval_email() fires unconditionally inside /auth/register, regardless of approval state. Look at the code we have — line ~135 of auth_router.py:
python

try:
    send_approval_email(
        to=body.email,
        name=body.name,
        userid=public_uuid,
        temp_password=temp_password,
    )

That's called on every register, not just when is_first_user. That's the bug. The temp password email should only go out when an admin approves.

Bug 2: Empty email on actual approval. When you clicked Approve in /admin, approve_application() calls send_approval_email() with temp_password = user.get("temp_password", "") — but at that point temp_password was ALREADY consumed during first-login (line 308 of auth_router clears it: "temp_password": ""). So the email goes out with an empty password field. The user already set their password — the "approve" email is now meaningless.

Bug 3: One-account-per-human enforcement. Right now auth_router has no email-uniqueness check. Same email could register multiple accounts.

Three fixes in one patch:

    Remove the inline send_approval_email call from /auth/register. Only fire on admin approve.
    In approve_application, don't send if temp_password is already empty (user has finished setup) — show a "user already finished onboarding" message instead. Or skip email entirely once approved.
    In /auth/register, before creating user, check if email_to_uuid:{email} exists. If yes → reject with "An account with this email already exists."

Run this to confirm the current state of /auth/register and /auth/approve:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '95,165p' /app/services/auth_router.py
docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '230,290p' /app/services/auth_router.py

Send both. I'll write a clean patch script that fixes all three. After that, the flow is:

    Apply → goes to pending queue silently, no email
    Admin approves → temp-password email fires (only once)
    User does first-login → password set, status=active
    Same email tries to register again → 409 with clear message

Send the two grep outputs.

Wiring Ms. Allis into the portal — let's plan before we build.

You currently have:

    Ms. Allis backend (jarvis-main-brain) on port 8050 with the full pipeline (21 LLMs → judges → BBB → synthesizer)
    Constitutional/BBB/health endpoints exposed via Caddy
    Token-based auth with redteam:token:* and redteam:quota:* schema
    60K geospatial features, 16K local resources, 1M address points, GBIM beliefs, spiritual RAG

What needs to happen for that "Talk to Ms. Allis" card to become a real chat:

    Endpoint — The signed-in user's bearer token (rt_…) needs to be accepted by jarvis-main-brain. What's the current chat endpoint shape? /chat? /v1/chat/completions? POST with {message, user_id}?
    Token auth on main-brain — It already uses redteam:token:* records, which is exactly what TokenService.issue_token writes. So the same bearer token from sign-in should work. Need to verify.
    Quota — Each token has a redteam:quota:* hash with total_requests/total_blocked. We probably want to surface that on the portal too ("you've sent X messages, Y were blocked").
    UI — Replace the "coming soon" card with a chat panel: message history list + textarea + send button + streaming response display. Quota counter + UEID identifier shown alongside.
    Routing — Caddy needs to route /chat* requests from the frontend through to main-brain on 8050.

Run this so I can see the chat endpoint shape:
bash

docker exec jarvis-main-brain python3 -c "
import json
import urllib.request
try:
    resp = urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8050/openapi.json', timeout=5)
    spec = json.loads(resp.read())
    for path, methods in spec.get('paths', {}).items():
        for method in methods:
            print(f'  {method.upper():5s} {path}')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'no openapi: {e}')
"

That dumps every endpoint. Send the output. Then I'll know exactly what the chat panel needs to call, and we'll know which existing endpoint vs what we need to add.

Plus — what's already accessible? Try this test from your terminal to see if the chat endpoint accepts a bearer token already:
bash

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" --data-urlencode "password=$YOUR_PASSWORD" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8050/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Hello Ms. Allis"}' | head -50

(Substitute your real password for $YOUR_PASSWORD or set read -s -p "pw: " YOUR_PASSWORD; echo first.)

Now the Ms. Allis wiring. Run these so I can see what's there:
bash

# What endpoints does jarvis-main-brain expose?
docker exec jarvis-main-brain python3 -c "
import json, urllib.request
try:
    spec = json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8050/openapi.json', timeout=5).read())
    for path, methods in spec.get('paths', {}).items():
        for method in methods:
            print(f'  {method.upper():5s} {path}')
except Exception as e:
    print(f'no openapi: {e}')
"

# Try a chat call with your existing token
read -s -p "your password: " PW; echo
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" --data-urlencode "password=$PW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin).get('access_token',''))")
echo "Token: ${TOKEN:0:15}..."

# Try common endpoint shapes
for path in /chat /api/chat /v1/chat /chat/completions /query; do
  echo "--- $path ---"
  curl -s -o /tmp/probe.txt -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
    -X POST "http://localhost:8050$path" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"message":"hello","query":"hello","prompt":"hello"}'
  head -c 200 /tmp/probe.txt 2>/dev/null
  echo
done

Claude responded: Ms.

Ms. Allis answered. POST /chat with {"message": "..."}, returns {"response": "..."}, accepts bearer tokens. That's the contract.

Plus you have async (/chat/async + /chat/status/{job_id}) which is better for long pipelines — 21 LLMs sequential could take 100+ seconds. Async means the UI doesn't hang.

Couple things to confirm before I build the chat panel:
bash

# What's the exact request/response shape for sync /chat?

import json, urllib.request

for endpoint in ['/chat', '/chat/async', '/chat/status/{job_id}']:
    print(f'=== {endpoint} ===')
    print(json.dumps(spec['paths'][endpoint], indent=2)[:1500])
    print()
"

# Does main-brain validate the bearer token via redteam:token:* lookup?
docker exec jarvis-main-brain grep -rn "redteam:token\|Bearer\|verify_token\|HTTPBearer" /app/services/ /app/main.py 2>/dev/null | head -20

# Check if Caddy routes /chat through to main-brain (should — earlier we saw /chat* → 18018, but main-brain is 8050)
sudo grep -A2 "/chat" /etc/caddy/Caddyfile | head -20

Claude responded: Got everything I need.

Got everything I need. Three things to handle before building the chat panel:

1. Schema — I need the Pydantic models. Run:
bash

docker exec jarvis-main-brain python3 -c "
import json, urllib.request
spec = json.loads(urllib.request.urlopen('http://localhost:8050/openapi.json', timeout=5).read())
for name in ['UltimateRequest','UltimateResponse','ChatJobRequest']:
    s = spec['components']['schemas'].get(name, {})
    print(f'=== {name} ===')
    print(json.dumps(s, indent=2))
    print()
"

2. Auth gateway — ms_jarvis_unified_gateway.py line 154 does rc.hgetall(f"redteam:token:{token}"), which is exactly the schema TokenService writes. Good news: when carrie_kidd signs in, the rt_<24hex> token IS a valid main-brain credential — same Redis key namespace. The bearer token from /auth/login should authenticate against main-brain too. We just need to confirm whether main-brain is fronted by the unified gateway or hit directly.

3. Caddy routing — needs surgery. Currently three rules collide:

/chat/status* → 8092 (auth-api — wrong, this is the legacy port the auth_router tunnel was on, not Ms. Allis)
/chat*        → forward_auth + 8055 (legacy auth)
/chat*        → 18018 (something else, probably the gateway)

For the portal to talk to Ms. Allis, /chat, /chat/async, /chat/status/* all need to go to port 8050 (jarvis-main-brain) with the bearer token preserved. We'll need to rewrite those Caddy rules.

But — let's avoid touching Caddy if possible. Check whether the frontend can hit main-brain through cloudflared directly:
bash

# Does main-brain have its own subdomain in cloudflared?
sudo grep -B1 -A2 "8050\|jarvis\|main-brain\|ai\." /etc/cloudflared/config.yml

# What's the unified gateway on port 18018?
docker ps --format '{{.Names}} {{.Ports}}' | grep -E '18018|8050'

# Can we hit chat through any existing public path right now?
curl -s -o /tmp/probe.txt -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" \
  -X POST https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hi"}'
head -c 300 /tmp/probe.txt; echo

Send all three — schemas + cloudflared config + the chat.mountainshares probe.

Once I have those, the build is straightforward:

    Frontend: replace the "coming soon" card with a real chat component (textarea, message list, async job poll)
    Routing: pick the cleanest path — either add /chat to egeria's tunnel route, repoint Caddy rules, OR have the frontend hit chat.mountainshares.us directly via CORS
    Auth: bearer token from sessionStorage, pass through

Probably 200 lines of frontend code, one Caddy edit, working chat in your portal in about 30 minutes.

Claude responded: Got everything I need.

Got everything I need. Plan is now concrete:

Routing. chat.mountainshares.us tunnel → :80 → Caddy chat.mountainshares.us → forwards to whatever's on 18018 (the unified gateway), which validates the bearer token (redteam:token:* lookup at line 154/1443) and forwards to main-brain on 8050. The 401 you got is exactly right — proves the auth gateway works. Send a valid token and it'll let you through.

So the frontend can call https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat directly from the portal page, with the bearer token from sessionStorage. No Caddy edits needed. The /chat path on egeria's site never gets touched.

One unknown — CORS. chat.mountainshares.us probably needs to allow egeria.mountainshares.us as an origin. Test:
bash

read -s -p "your password: " PW; echo
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" --data-urlencode "password=$PW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

# Real chat call to confirm token works end-to-end through the public hostname
curl -s -X POST https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"What can you tell me about Mount Hope, West Virginia?","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 800

# CORS preflight from a hypothetical browser at egeria
curl -sI -X OPTIONS https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat \
  -H "Origin: https://egeria.mountainshares.us" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type" | grep -iE "access-control|HTTP"

Send both.

If the first returns a real Ms. Allis answer with services_used, consciousness_level, etc. — perfect, the contract is locked. If the second returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://egeria.mountainshares.us (or *) — no Caddy edit needed. If not — small Caddy edit to add the CORS header for that origin.

Once both pass, I write the chat panel. Async (/chat/async + poll /chat/status/{job_id}) since responses can take 100+ seconds with the full pipeline. Replaces the "coming soon" card with conversation history, textarea, send button, "Ms. Allis is thinking..." indicator, and shows services_used + processing_time as small metadata under each response (so red-team can see what fired).

Send the two outputs.

Claude responded: Two issues:

Two issues:

1. Status is denied — that's why login fails (auth_router probably rejects non-active users). You must've clicked Deny in the admin panel by accident at some point. Fix it back to active:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
r.hset('user:dbc4ee80-3149-4873-8762-84cb81b64fd3', 'status', 'active')
print('status:', r.hget('user:dbc4ee80-3149-4873-8762-84cb81b64fd3', 'status'))
"

2. Indent error — the leading 3-space indentation in front of every line broke Python. Run this version with no extra spaces:
bash

read -s -p "new password (10+ chars): " NEWPW; echo
docker exec -e PW="$NEWPW" jarvis-auth-api python3 -c "
import bcrypt, redis, os
r = redis.from_url(os.environ['REDIS_URL'], decode_responses=True)
for k in r.scan_iter('user:*'):
    if r.hget(k, 'proposed_userid') == 'carrie_kidd':
        r.hset(k, 'password_hash', bcrypt.hashpw(os.environ['PW'].encode(), bcrypt.gensalt()).decode())
        print(f'reset for {k}')
"

Then test login + chat:
bash

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" --data-urlencode "password=$NEWPW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")
echo "Token: ${TOKEN:0:15}..."

curl -s -X POST https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"What can you tell me about Mount Hope, West Virginia?","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 1500

        import bcrypt
        username_clean = username.strip().lower()

        # If not found, scan for matching proposed_userid (UUID-keyed records)

Claude responded: There it is.

There it is. /auth/login does NOT call tok.authenticate(). It has its own inline implementation that does its own user lookup + bcrypt check, and it's failing — that's why the 401.

Need to see lines 175-200 (the part you didn't show) to see the actual lookup/bcrypt logic:
bash

docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '175,200p' /app/services/auth_router.py

Send that. The bug is one of:

    Different lookup pattern (e.g. constructs user:{username} directly, doesn't scan)
    Different bcrypt invocation
    Status check that rejects despite status=active

Once I see it, I write a clean replacement that calls tok.authenticate() (which works perfectly, as we just proved) and uses the result. Single function rewrite.
You said: (crypto-venv) cakidd@cakidd-Legion-5-16IRX9:~/msjarvis-rebuild-working/ms-allis-frontend$ docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '175,200p' /app/services/auth_rout…

(crypto-venv) cakidd@cakidd-Legion-5-16IRX9:~/msjarvis-rebuild-working/ms-allis-frontend$ docker exec jarvis-auth-api sed -n '175,200p' /app/services/auth_[router.py](http://router.py)
@auth_[router.post](http://router.post)("/login", response_model=TokenResponse)
async def login(form: Annotated[OAuth2PasswordRequestForm, Depends()]):
    _, tok, r = _get_services()
    # OAuth2 form sends 'username' field — we treat it as email
    email = form.username
    public_uuid = r.get(f"email_to_uuid:{email}")
    if not public_uuid:
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Invalid username or password",
            headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
        )
    user = r.hgetall(f"user:{public_uuid}")
    if not user:
        raise HTTPException(status_code=401, detail="Invalid username or password")
    import bcrypt
    stored_hash = user.get("password_hash", "")
    if not stored_hash or not bcrypt.checkpw(form.password.encode(), stored_hash.encode()):
        raise HTTPException(
            status_code=status.HTTP_401_UNAUTHORIZED,
            detail="Invalid username or password",
            headers={"WWW-Authenticate": "Bearer"},
        )
    userid = user.get("proposed_userid", public_uuid)

Claude responded: Got it.

Got it. /auth/login treats username as email and looks up email_to_uuid:{email}. You typed carrie_kidd, no email lookup matches, 401. The frontend was always sending username=carrie_kidd to log in — that worked when authenticate() was called, but now there's an inline implementation that wants email.

Two ways to fix. Either:

A. Change login to call tok.authenticate() — single source of truth, the scan handles both email and proposed_userid.

B. Make the inline login accept either email OR userid — small change, keeps the file structure.

A is cleaner. Let me write it:
Created a file, ran a command, read a file
bash

bash ~/Downloads/fix_login_endpoint.sh

What it does:

    Backs up auth_router.py and token_service.py to ~/.ms-allis-backups/login-fix-<ts>/
    Replaces the broken inline /auth/login body with a call to tok.authenticate() — same code that we already proved works
    Extends authenticate() to also match by email (so red-team users typing their email address into "Username" field will also work)
    Restarts auth-api
    Tests login with carrie_kidd (proposed_userid) AND with kiddstechnical@gmail.com (email) — both should succeed
    Tests the chat call with the resulting token — should return a real Ms. Allis answer about Mount Hope

Send the output. Once both logins return access_token AND chat returns a 200 with response/services_used, I build the chat panel for the portal.
Fix login endpoint
SH

Claude responded: 502 means the chain broke somewhere between Cloudflare and main-brain.

502 means the chain broke somewhere between Cloudflare and main-brain. Could be: cloudflared route, Caddy, gateway on 18018, or main-brain itself. Trace each layer:
bash

# Layer 1: main-brain direct (bypass everything)
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8050/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hi","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 600

# Layer 2: gateway on 18018
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:18018/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hi","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 600

# Layer 3: Caddy as chat.mountainshares.us
curl -s -X POST -H "Host: chat.mountainshares.us" http://127.0.0.1:80/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"hi","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 600

# Are all the services actually up?

Apr 27
Claude responded: Both empty.

Both empty. Gateway has no exposed ports, nothing listens on 18018. Caddy's been proxying to a dead address.

Fix: route chat.mountainshares.us directly to main-brain on :8050. Skip the gateway. Main-brain validates the bearer token itself (we just proved it works).
bash

# Check current chat.mountainshares.us route
sudo grep -B2 -A5 "chat.mountainshares" /etc/caddy/Caddyfile

# Make the swap
sudo sed -i 's|reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:18018|reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8050|g' /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo systemctl reload caddy

# Verify route is now :8050
sudo grep "8050\|18018" /etc/caddy/Caddyfile

# Re-test public chat
curl -s -X POST https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"What can you tell me about Mount Hope, West Virginia?","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}' \
  -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 1200

Send all of that. Should return 200 and a real response.

Caveat — if $TOKEN is the older one (rt_4bbb587116217abebe25bf5d), it should still be valid (24-hour expiry). If you closed your shell, log in fresh:

echo "${TOKEN:0:15}..."

Two architecture decisions before I write code — quick answer needed:

1. Sync vs async? Sync (/chat) blocks for 47 seconds (your test). Async (/chat/async + poll /chat/status/{job_id}) keeps the UI responsive — user sees "Ms. Allis is thinking..." while the request runs. Async is the right call for a 21-LLM pipeline.

2. Show pipeline metadata to red-team? Each response includes services_used, processing_time, consciousness_level, truthverdict. Red-team needs to see this — it's the whole point. Champions probably don't. I'll show it as a collapsible "Show pipeline details" caret under each response, role-gated to whoever has admin (red-team alpha) and toggleable for everyone else later.

3. Conversation history — keep messages in React state for the session, or persist via the chat_history:{userid} Redis pattern that probably already exists somewhere in main-brain? Session-only is faster to build; persistence is a follow-up.

Claude responded: CORS — header is duplicated (, ).

CORS — header is duplicated (*, *). Likely two layers (Caddy + main-brain) both setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, browser sees the doubled header and rejects. We saw this earlier when we did the OPTIONS probe and got two access-control-allow-origin: * lines.

Easiest fix: stop one of them from setting it. Caddy is probably the one we control. Check:
bash

sudo grep -n "Access-Control" /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
curl -sI -X OPTIONS https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat/async \
  -H "Origin: https://egeria.mountainshares.us" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: authorization,content-type" | grep -i "access-control"

# Are duplicates from Caddy or main-brain?
curl -sI -H "Origin: https://egeria.mountainshares.us" http://127.0.0.1:8050/chat/async \
  -X OPTIONS \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" | grep -i "access-control"

Send all three. The first shows what Caddy is set to do; the second shows the public response (with Cloudflare/Caddy added); the third shows what main-brain says directly.

Whichever layer is doubling, we make it not. Probably 2-line edit and Caddy reload.

Apr 27
Claude responded: Found it.

Found it. Two issues:

1. Caddy's header directive at the site-level applies on every response. But the matched handle /chat* block also adds the upstream's CORS header (because Caddy passes through what main-brain returns... wait, main-brain doesn't return one). So why two?

Actually — looking more carefully — the header directive in Caddy v2 adds to existing headers by default. So when proxying to upstream, if upstream returns its own Access-Control-Allow-Origin, you get both. But you said main-brain returns none. Let me look elsewhere.

2. More likely: /chat* has forward_auth 127.0.0.1:8055. That's the dead auth port. The forward_auth call probably fails, AND the response from that 8055 attempt returns its own CORS headers (or the failure path does). Plus the site-level header directive adds its own. = two *s.

Two fixes that are simple. Either:

Fix A: Remove the broken forward_auth. Main-brain validates tokens itself, we don't need Caddy to pre-validate.
bash

sudo cp /etc/caddy/Caddyfile /tmp/Caddyfile.before-cors-fix

# Remove the forward_auth block from /chat* handler
sudo python3 - <<'PYEOF'
import re
path = "/etc/caddy/Caddyfile"
src = open(path).read()
old = """	handle /chat* {
		forward_auth 127.0.0.1:8055 {
			uri /validate
			copy_headers X-Userid
		}
		reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8050 {
			header_up Authorization {http.request.header.Authorization}
			header_up X-API-Key {http.request.header.X-Api-Key}
			header_up X-Userid {http.request.header.X-Userid}
		}
	}"""
new = """	handle /chat* {
		reverse_proxy 127.0.0.1:8050 {
			header_up Authorization {http.request.header.Authorization}
		}
	}"""
if old in src:
    open(path, "w").write(src.replace(old, new))
    print("forward_auth removed from /chat* handler")
else:
    print("anchor not found, no change made")
PYEOF

# Force-set CORS so it's not duplicated by upstream
sudo sed -i 's|Access-Control-Allow-Origin "\*"|Access-Control-Allow-Origin "*"\n\t\tdefer|' /etc/caddy/Caddyfile

sudo caddy validate --config /etc/caddy/Caddyfile
sudo systemctl reload caddy

# Verify only ONE access-control-allow-origin now
curl -sI -X OPTIONS https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat/async \
  -H "Origin: https://egeria.mountainshares.us" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" | grep -i "access-control"

The defer directive in Caddy makes the header directive run AFTER the proxy returns, replacing rather than appending. That, plus removing the dead forward_auth, should leave exactly one *.

Send the output. After it shows ONE access-control-allow-origin: *, hard-refresh /portal and try the chat again.

Quick command-line probe to confirm the endpoint actually accepts our payload:
bash

TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  --data-urlencode "username=carrie_kidd" --data-urlencode "password=$NEWPW" \
  http://localhost:8092/auth/login | python3 -c "import json,sys; print(json.load(sys.stdin)['access_token'])")

# Submit async job
JOB=$(curl -s -X POST https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat/async \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"message":"Hello Ms. Allis","user_id":"carrie_kidd"}')
echo "submit response: $JOB"

JOBID=$(echo "$JOB" | python3 -c "import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('job_id',''))" 2>/dev/null)
echo "job_id: $JOBID"

# Poll once
if [ -n "$JOBID" ]; then
  sleep 3
  curl -s "https://chat.mountainshares.us/chat/status/$JOBID" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
    -w "\nHTTP %{http_code}\n" | head -c 600
fi

Send the curl output + Network tab info. The curl tells me what shape the response has (we may have wrong field names in chat.ts — e.g. id vs job_id, or status uses different progress fields).
